item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image originates from here, whose domain has expired. As such, we could not verify if it was released under CC 3.0 or not, and thus is assumed to be not compliant and therefore removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could be better in a couple ways, but I actually have little to complain as is.

I can't understand why people is si upset by it being weak. Yes, it is just a list of D&D cantrips, and far from the power of most skips.

But it is also a crash course on magic that truly works, and works quite fast! Just 96 pages worth of reading, some of those with diagrams and stuff, so it isn't even so much actual written content. And by the description, it isn't any particularly hard reading, either.

At the end, the person is able to conjure quite a number of effects. And though those are weak at D&D context, just think about them at a IRL context. The person that studies it won't be a world-breaking monstrosity, but will still be an SCP on its own right. Depending on the person, a dangerous one, by the way. And the books can teach this to anyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would benefit from more flavor, less crunch and a stronger focus on being the 'base" of something larger.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

I sure do like this article, both for the joke of D&D books as spellbooks, and for the eluding that there is more advanced and dangerous anomalous effects to be had in the expansions. Who is to say that something along the lines of SCP-2336 or such thumatology as that covered in one of the GOC lectures would be found in those elusive expansions. As such, I am proposing the following edits:

Changing this:

The books imply that stronger effects can be invoked using information that can be found in a different, more advanced set of books. However, no such volumes have yet been found.

To this:

The books imply that stronger effects can be invoked using information that can be found in a different, more advanced set of books. However, no such volumes have yet been found.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a big fan of SCP and D&D, this one is pretty lackluster all around. Its one of the most bland and boring articles I've read. There are more interesting objects on the Anomalous Object log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EXTREEEMLY LATE but it's blue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly can't tell whether this is your bog-standard spellbook with a coat of nostalgia and pop culture applied to it, or an attempt to SCP-ify Christian panic regarding D&D. Either way, though, I'm not particularly impressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, of course. This is, as it said, the Basic Set. The young apprentice's training spells. This is useful in the same way that a child-sized bicycle is:

"With enough practice, you'll eventually be able to upgrade to the Big Stuff. And then you can pull tricks that will stun and impress everyone, and may one day save your life."

Now, if someone were to get good at these, then find the next volume? Or the next several? This is the beginning for training a Wizard in the real world. Honestly, one of the cooler and scarier SCPs I've seen yet. Just imagine what could happen if a sufficiently powerful Evil aligned Wizard was trained…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CANTRIPS!? All of D&D and we only get 0 level spells!? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a fan of D&D, I can attest that in true Dungeons and Dragons fashion…all the good stuff is in expansions lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, the Wicca Module. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno why I didn't like this the first time around. Okay, that's a lie, I know why I didn't like it — my tastes have just changed to the point where I can't fully empathize. It's nifty.

Now, moving on to AN EXTREMELY IMPORTANT QUESTION: what color is the crayon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking at this again, I'm not sure why I was "So disappointed," but this could be better.
The spells lack depth… OK, so I'm a HUGE RPer, and one thing I enjoy is taking the "flat" descriptions in RPG books and look at them in a "real world." Starting with gestures, words, and materials consumed in the spell (which should all give very, very good clues to the SCP's provenance),and then looking at the peripheral effects (glow, odor, temperature shift, etc.). Most important in SCP, are there any subtle fringe effects? Localized probability distortions (perhaps relating to the dice?) and the like might give this SCP more texture and depth.
This is exciting because it is wish fulfillment, but every role-player knows that wishes are dangerous things that are never granted EXACTLY as you wished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's stupidly late and I'm kinda sick 'n zony, but I loved this so much I had to say something, then reread, and was so disappointed I had to say something. Then I just started writing and realized I should no-vote and post a comment with clarity and lucidity at a later date.
Kudos on concept; dunno if I like the delivery yet. I am sure it could be better. Never a good sign when the suspenseful moment between the title and seeing the photo is the best part, followed by the joy of seeing the photo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seriously love this and its framing as a "beginning level" sort of book, as it hints to. Not sure why so many people are disappointed. I'm sure they'd probably want you to jump to the third-level book or something along those lines.

It smacks of 1st level, beginner D&D. Slightly useful spells which are slowly modified and upgraded to unleash their full potential: Protective circle that eventually has effects, being able to project more than an "itch" or "tickle", pulling much larger beasties out of the air. The potential of the powers that the article alludes to leaves quite enough to the imagination, and I've found much pleasure in ruminating about what might come in the next book. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm new here so take my comment with a shaker of salt
I personally think it could use an addendum mentioning an ongoing search for those other 4 levels is being conducted. Something like, in the catalog there's a check next to the basic set and the expert one is circled, something that specifically mentions the existence of 5 sets total would kinda add to the whole foreboding that coulda been there but was cut off riiight before it got to the surface.

that being said, I thought it was a good SCP, not really missing much and well written but just lacking that big punch at the end that it almost has.
just my 2¢

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like a D&D item. Not literally a D&D item, I mean… oh, you know.

It would be nice if there was some depth to this, is all, because it doesn't work for me as just a wizarding handbook with false advertising.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, upvote. You had me at the picture. Some of the criticism I earlier I feel is valid, but…
C'mon! D&D as an SCP! I had to give you my upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, sulfur is a common ingredient in alchemy. Any connection to "fire and brimstone" is coincidental.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Making it Satanic in nature would have imposed religiosity on it (since Satan is specifically a Judeo-Christian concept) that, frankly, would not have worked at all.

In that case, you might want to remove the smell of sulfur.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this.Its not over the top,open a door to hell kind of thing.Its simple stuff just like the real books are.Hope to find the more advanced books soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the basic set (I still have mine around here somewhere). It makes sense that the Foundation would be looking for the more advanced sets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^_^ I'm assuming that the list of effects is intentionally similar to the cantrips in Unearthed Arcana?

It's entertaining, although I'll agree with the others that this is only anomalous in that the researchers haven't ascertained why the various spells work. Otherwise, it's pretty harmless and more than a tad useful. I've got no vote up or down. I don't particularly feel like it's a good example of a SCP object, but I also think it has its place that's only satisfied by getting a full entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I think the most entertainment this could make is if you gave it to actual D&D players. Or very bad ones, at the least.

"Repairing minor damage an object has sustained"

"Nyeeeh, oh mah god Jake you broke my mom's vase!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Provided that it's proven safe for long-term use, I imagine that the Foundation would love to have a task force trained in some of this. Especially the protective circle and repair spells.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see devil worship and suicide and demons here…

…but maybe I'm just not looking hard enough. >.>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like those Chick Tracts were right after all…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: quik you are the best math nerd <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is the aforementioned photographic evidence. Click Files and click the file name to view the image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: QuikngruvnQuikngruvn, what's with the file upload?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
None at all, wishun. I have a bit of a backstory in my head (enough to justify some of the details), but not yet enough to make a tale out of. Heck, the advanced sets may not even exist— they may just have been planned, but never completed. Rah ambiguity.

Also, the fact that SCP-210 is at +25 makes me giggle madly. (Not that I want it stuck there, since I have the photo evidence.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quik, you mention the escalating rules sets, does that imply you have plans to expand the concept? I could see that being an interesting basis for a story (although I think not neccessarily a good SCP entry). Maybe even tie in 433 as the Master or Immortal level book, the workign ritual would be something like "Planar Travel".

Also, retro D&D reference= reflexive upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Magic Missile!" *random kid explodes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember kids, no using SCPs to simulate real danger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
moose, I thought about it, and I still want to stay away from using actual D&D spell names. I think they would detract more than they'd add, IMO.

I'm not really sure what they'd be detracting, and I think since you're using actual D&D material, why not use actual spell names? Still, a +19 rating is more than enough for you to happily ignore that feedback - clearly, while I don't like it, plenty of others do. :) (And as I said, it's an excellent concept.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no horror in SCP-914, SCP-131 or SCP-994. Being interesting, IMHO, is the biggest requirement for a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the two effects you added are enough to negate my former comment. it is now satisfactory. upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the context of the culture that spawned and perpetuates this myth, witchcraft and satanism are one and the same. That is, it's a fundamentalist christian legend, not a wiccan one. I was mostly looking for a way to inject some horror into a rather tame scp.

Right now, this is Something You Would Want To Have, which doesn't lend itself very well to being a proper scp. Where's the horror? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, I had given this about even odds that it was going to be downvoted to oblivion for being too silly, so having it reach double digits is a pleasant surprise, to say the least. Turns out that trying to describe a magic spell without using either "magic" or "spell" is hard to do. Also, I wasn't consciously trying to, but I managed to not expunge anything, for whatever it's worth.

moose, I thought about it, and I still want to stay away from using actual D&D spell names. I think they would detract more than they'd add, IMO. 'Sides, Robo-Dude has that covered already.

Sal, um… an object that lets you summon a critter out of thin air is "mundane"? Or is that just by Foundation standards, which… I can't really argue. But Rhett's got in it that this is, really, the Basic Set. Figure, IRL there were eventually five sets of rules for D&D for increasingly-powerful characters (Basic, Expert, Companion, Master, and Immortal), so there may very well be five analogous volumes of spell books…. But, you gotta crawl before you can walk, and so I didn't want to make the effects here at all powerful.

Kerlyssa, D&D was also supposedly a gateway to witchcraft and the occult, in addition to Satanism, and I decided to go with magic in general, from… some unspecified source. Making it Satanic in nature would have imposed religiosity on it (since Satan is specifically a Judeo-Christian concept) that, frankly, would not have worked at all.

Ker/Eric, I can certainly see how this could be a -J… but if I were trying to make it a -J, I would have gone for over the top instead of subdued. As it is, it's right where it belongs (or will be, once it gets its number).

Severe, crap, I had totally forgotten about 433 when I wrote this up. I think mine is different enough from yours to stand on its own, but I can definitely see the similarities.

And as always, thanks to everyone for reading, voting, and commenting.

(That turned out longer than expected. Ah well.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this and upvoted it. That being said, it doesn't reaaally feel like it's right for the Foundation…I can't really explain why, it just seems….off. Also, to be a bit self-serving, it is very similar to one of the ones I wrote, SCP-433 in that it is a book containing instructions for performing rituals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, it is a delightful retelling of a popular meme, but it feels like it should be a -J. Much like the 'Mascot creature that somehow swallowed a live human,' the flavor of the text smacks of over-protectiveness.

As a gamer, I love the idea, but this one gives me a chuckle instead of a chill.

No vote at this time, "New York Abstains, courteously."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It's an actual modern urban legend. However, considering it is an urban legend about a game being satanic, and this is a horror site, why not have it do something more…interesting? It reads like a joke scp at the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recognize this isn't about what this currently DOES do, but what it has the potential to do…..but that potential isn't quite enough to satisfy the reader in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. This is too mundane even for an object that is supposed to be underwhelming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this idea. But, as I said in chat, I find the execution disappointing and the effects a list of boring stuff that may as well not even be there. It's possibly my familiarity with D&D and my familiarity with the "D&D is Satanic" craze of the 80s, but still, I really think this could easily be much better. It would be an improvement for me even if you just used actual D&D spell names and gave them effects as mundane as the ones already here ("Fireball" makes a tiny, lighter-flame-size ball of fire, "Magic Missile" makes an effect similar to a sparkler, etc).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love you, Quik.
